one_line_takeaway: A collaborative offline-LLM majority-vote pipeline can filter 65k+ Linux packages for cryptographic relevance from metadata alone, reaching F1 0.86 after prompt optimization and matching cloud LLMs, but rigorous statistical analysis shows the 5-model ensemble's real independence is only ~1.6 effective votes (rho ~= 0.52), capping the achievable gains from adding more models.
key_finding: First-iteration (unoptimized prompts) local-model performance was weak: majority vote scored Acc 0.73/Spec 0.77/Prec 0.74/Recall 0.70/F1 0.72, and was actually beaten by the single best local model, llama, at F1 0.77 (Table 2) -- i.e., majority voting averaged out rather than amplified performance at this stage. Cloud models on the same 390-package ground truth outperformed local models but only modestly: cloud majority vote reached F1 0.86 (Acc 0.86, Spec 0.84, Prec 0.84, Recall 0.87; Table 3), a 3-9 point F1 gap over the best local model (llama, F1 0.77). After prompt optimization (refined parsing, re-querying malformed responses at higher temperature, per-model-tailored prompts), local-model performance improved substantially: majority vote reached Acc 0.85/Spec 0.75/Prec 0.78/Recall 0.95/F1 0.86 (Table 4), now matching the cloud-model majority vote and outperforming every individual local model (best individual: deepseek at F1 0.84). Response-quality/error-rate analysis (Table 1) showed no correlation between model size and JSON-parsing reliability: the smallest model, phi (2.1GB), had the lowest error rate (72 invalid/65,222 valid, 0.11%), while the largest, gpt4all (6.9GB), had the highest (1,137 invalid, 1.74%); 1,765 packages (2.70%) were dropped entirely due to at least one unparsable model response. Independence analysis found the 5 models' votes are strongly correlated (rho ~= 0.52, design effect Deff ~= 3.096, effective sample size neff ~= 1.615 out of nominal n=5), and the authors derive analytically that effective sample size asymptotes to only 2 as n grows under this correlation level (neff = 2n/(n+1) -> 2), concluding that adding models beyond 3-5 offers little benefit unless they are substantially more independent. The final 3-model ensemble (deepseek, phi, mistral, selected by recall-weighted score) achieved 5-fold cross-validated F1 = 0.82 (mean; sigma=0.059), Recall 0.86 (sigma=0.075), Acc 0.82 (sigma=0.056) -- Table 5 -- described by the authors as 'internal validation rather than evaluation on an independent test set.'
core_contribution: The paper (Hirsch, Raab, Bauer, Loebenberger; OTH Amberg / Fraunhofer AISEC) proposes and empirically evaluates a collaborative-LLM heuristic pipeline for building cryptographic asset inventories (a precursor to a Cryptographic Bill of Materials, CBOM) from Linux distribution package metadata, aimed at supporting post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration and crypto-agility. The pipeline (Fig. 1) takes package name/description/first-level dependencies from a package manager, queries n independently deployed LLMs with an engineered prompt asking for a JSON verdict on cryptographic relevance, and aggregates the n answers via majority vote (>= floor(n/2)+1 agree). The authors run this on 65,295 deduplicated Fedora Linux packages (collected via dnf, plus some Docker/VirtualBox/VS Code packages) using five locally hosted models (GPT4All-13B-Snoozy, Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Nous-Hermes-2-Mistral-7B-DPO, Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct, and DeepSeek R1 via Ollama) chosen specifically for on-premises/offline privacy preservation. They then statistically characterize inter-model agreement (chi-squared goodness-of-fit against binomial and beta-binomial distributions; design-effect/effective-sample-size analysis), manually label a stratified 390-package ground-truth sample, benchmark local vs. four cloud models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash/Pro, Mistral Codestral-2508), iterate on prompt engineering, and finally select an optimal 3-of-5 local-model subset (deepseek, phi, mistral) via a recall-weighted score and 5-fold cross-validation. The output is proposed as a first-pass filter to reduce manual crypto-asset discovery workload before deeper code-level CBOM generation.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the adversarial-ML sense; this is a defensive-tooling/asset-discovery paper. The 'threat' motivating the work is organizational: undetected classical (non-PQC) cryptography in large, heterogeneous software estates that will become vulnerable to future quantum-computing attacks, combined with the infeasibility of manual or single-language static-analysis inventories at scale (65k+ packages). A secondary constraint treated as a first-class design requirement (not a threat per se) is data confidentiality: the paper explicitly rejects sending proprietary package/dependency data to external LLM APIs and restricts its primary evaluated approach to on-premises, offline-hosted open-weight LLMs to avoid leaking enterprise software-composition information to third-party servers; cloud models (GPT-5, Gemini, Codestral) are used only as an external comparison baseline, not as part of the recommended deployment.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) reliance on package metadata (name/description/first-level dependencies) rather than source or binary code means packages with sparse or ambiguous public descriptions can be misclassified, and statically linked/vendored libraries or implicit crypto usage buried in transitive dependencies may be missed entirely; (2) the manual ground-truth labeling, while described as comprehensive, may not capture all cryptographically relevant nuances; (3) results are 'preliminary' per the abstract, and the 390-package cross-validation constitutes internal validation on the same pool used to tune the model selection, not evaluation on an independent held-out test set; (4) the majority-vote strategy's effectiveness is fundamentally capped by inter-model correlation (rho ~= 0.52), meaning the ensemble's real statistical power is much smaller than its nominal size suggests; (5) local-model inference is resource-intensive (a single query took ~4 minutes on a developer notebook vs. 2-20 seconds on a 7xA100 DGX machine, and the DeepSeek/Ollama run took ~3 days for ~65-70k queries per model); (6) the study covers only Fedora Linux (though the authors argue the method generalizes to other package-manager ecosystems); (7) the approach is explicitly a first-pass filter, not a replacement for deeper cryptographic-primitive/algorithm-level analysis needed for a full CBOM.

datasets_used: 65,295 deduplicated Fedora Linux packages (name, description, first-level dependencies) collected via dnf from Fedora/RPM Fusion repos plus Docker, VirtualBox, and Visual Studio Code packages
63,529-package cleaned subset (after discarding 1,765 packages with at least one unparsable LLM response across the 5 local models)
390-package manually labeled stratified ground-truth sample (65 packages per true-vote-count stratum 0-5, 95% confidence / 5% margin of error)
quantitative_results: Table 1 response validity: phi 65,222/72 valid/invalid (0.11% error), deepseek 65,199/95 (0.15%), llama 65,094/200 (0.31%), mistral 64,974/320 (0.49%), gpt4all 64,157/1,137 (1.74%); aggregate 63,529/1,765 (2.70%)
Chi-squared goodness-of-fit vs. binomial: chi^2 ~= 612,668, df=3, critical value 16.266 at alpha=0.001 (rejected)
Chi-squared goodness-of-fit vs. beta-binomial: chi^2 ~= 767.63, df=2, critical value 13.82 at alpha=0.001 (rejected); fitted params alpha_hat=0.0751, beta_hat=0.8368
Design effect Deff ~= 3.096, effective sample size neff ~= 1.615 (out of n=5), estimated intra-class correlation rho ~= 0.52
Table 2 (pre-optimization, local models): majority Acc 0.73/Spec 0.77/Prec 0.74/Recall 0.70/F1 0.72; llama (best individual) F1 0.77; phi F1 0.76; mistral F1 0.70; deepseek F1 0.66; gpt4all F1 0.53
Table 3 (cloud models, same 390-package ground truth): majority Acc 0.86/Spec 0.84/Prec 0.84/Recall 0.87/F1 0.86; openai(gpt-5) F1 0.85; geminiflash F1 0.85; geminipro F1 0.83; codestral-2508 F1 0.80

Table 4 (post-prompt-optimization, local models): majority Acc 0.85/Spec 0.75/Prec 0.78/Recall 0.95/F1 0.86; deepseek (best individual) F1 0.84; llama F1 0.78; phi F1 0.76; mistral F1 0.74; gpt4all F1 0.53
Table 5 (5-fold CV, 3-model ensemble deepseek+phi+mistral): mean Acc 0.82 (sigma 0.056), Spec 0.79 (sigma 0.046), Prec 0.79 (sigma 0.050), Recall 0.86 (sigma 0.075), F1 0.82 (sigma 0.059)
Package agreement distribution (Fig. 2): 1,669 packages classified relevant by all 5 models; 51,965 packages with 0 true votes
Effective-sample-size asymptote: neff = 2n/(n+1) -> 2 as n -> infinity under rho ~= 0.52; neff ~= 1.5 with 3 models, ~= 1.67 with 5 models
baselines_compared: 5 local open-weight LLMs: GPT4All-13B-Snoozy (gpt4all-13b-snoozy-q4_0), Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct.Q4_0 (llama), Nous-Hermes-2-Mistral-7B-DPO.Q4_0 (mistral), Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct.Q4_0 (phi), DeepSeek R1 via Ollama (deepseek)
4 cloud/proprietary LLMs used only as external comparison: OpenAI GPT-5, Google Gemini 2.5 Flash, Google Gemini 2.5 Pro, Mistral AI Codestral-2508
Binomial and beta-binomial statistical distributions fitted to the empirical true-vote-count distribution as null models for the independence assumption
